speaker: interviewer
text: Today is January the 30th, 1966. --enough to eat to keep you going? Today is January the 30th, 1996. We are interviewing survivor Michael Steinstock-- M-I-C-H-A-E-L S-T-E-I-N-S-T-O-C.

speaker: interviewee
text: His original name is pronounced [? Shteinshtock ?] and spelled S- Z as in Zebra- T-A- J as in Jane- N-S- Z as in Zebra- T-O-K. The interviewer is Sally Friedman. And we are conducting the interview in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania in the United States of America. And we will be using English as our language. Today is January 30, 1996. The survivor being interviewed today is Michael Steinstock. I am Sally Friedman. And we are conducting this interview in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania in the United States of America. And the language that we're using is English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you please tell us your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Michael Steinstock.

speaker: interviewer
text: And would you mind spelling your name as it was originally spelled?

speaker: interviewee
text: Michael S-Z-T-A-J-N-S-Z-T-O-K.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sztajnsztok.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Steinstock, where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm born in Poland, Lublin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you spell that for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Poland I don't have to spell.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not Poland, right.

speaker: interviewee
text: L-U-B-L-I-N

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Lublin.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: May the 23rd, 1919.

speaker: interviewer
text: I-- I know that we're going to be taking you way back, but I wonder whether you could tell me a little bit about your earliest memories of life at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: I come from a very orthodox family. My father is a tailor and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a very poor man. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] I have two sisters. The first sister, the name is Serl, and the second name-- husband-- is Wisnia, W-I-S-N-I-A. The second sister is Sara, called here Sonya. The second name, from the husband, is Herscman-- H-E-R-S-C-M-A-N, Hercman.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rywka-- W-- R-Y-W-K-A. The second name is R-A-J-Z-L-A, Rajzla.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ah ha. And where are you in the family? Are you the youngest or oldest, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm the youngster.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Do you remember what your home felt like, looked like? What was it like to live there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I live in a one kitchen, no-- no windows [LAUGHS] in one room. When-- when mine middle sister get married, she lived with her husband in the room. I, my father, and mine mother lived in the kitchen. In the same kitchen, you cook, and sleep, and everything-- no windows, and no water, and no toilet.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it in a house or an apartment building?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's apartment, a very old-- about 150 year old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ah ha. So there were other families living near you, in the same building?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were the only family in that building?

speaker: interviewee
text: In this-- no, a lot of families there-- not my family. Now, a lot of families are there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: About 10 families.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ah ha.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I live of the street is the name Ruska-- R-U-S-K-A, Number 30, 3-0.

speaker: interviewer
text: You remember that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. I remember everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you mentioned that there were about 10 other families who lived there. Were most of them Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only Jewish. This is-- I live in a neighborhood-- 500 years ago is there a ghetto. And this was by the King Borislav [INAUDIBLE]. And this [INAUDIBLE] come like this-- about 500 years the same way. The people lived there-- Jewish people lived in this neighborhood. And Lublin-- in lived about 40,000 Jew. Yeah, a nice city. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was life easy and normal for Jewish people because you were-- there were so many?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the life was normal. And the people-- the Jewish people had a lot of crops, you know, growing crops. And they had unions, Jewish unions. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] you know, they have a theater, Jewish theater.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were your parents involved in some of these clubs? Or you mentioned they were very orthodox.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, my mother-- when I'm born, my mother 40 years old. And she gets sick of asthma.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry, with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Asthma.

speaker: interviewer
text: Asthma. Ah ha, ah ha.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my father's a very poor man, and they cannot afford to go somewhere. You understand what I mean? I remember [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] a lot of days I get up in the morning, no bread. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Now, my older sister had five sons-- the older sister. And she moved to Warsaw in 1930. She moved to Warsaw with her husband, with their five sons. The other sister lived together with my mother and father and with me. And she have a twin, a boy and a girl. And then, by Hitler, she born a other baby, a-- a son. When I'm not in the home, I'm in Russia at this time. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] What I can tell you-- my brother-in-law was live together with-- with my parents, and worked with my father to get a tailor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they have a shop, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, work for somebody, you know? And my father work only three months in the year. That's it. In the season.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the season, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What season did he work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tailor.

speaker: interviewer
text: But what's-- you said he worked only three months.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is he worked for the stores what saved clothes for wintertime and for spring.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: Summer time, nothing to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that he was very religious. In your home, how was religion practiced? Did you observe kosher?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very orthodox. Kosher-- this is normal. My father has the special uniform.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his uniform?

speaker: interviewee
text: A special black suit with a special hat. My mother wear a-- a wig, a sheitel. Jewish--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever see her without that wig? Did you ever see her without that wig?

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw her, yeah. Natural, I saw her. I'm the son. I can see. No, she had blonde hair. She's a very tall woman. I'm very happy my father is five foot, a little man and skinny-- a very skinny man. And my mother is tall and the-- no. And-- and the people-- and my father and mother loved our-- our-- one or the other-- a very nice couple.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you mentioned that sometimes you were hungry. You were very poor. Did you-- at that time, when-- early years, did relatives and friends help you sometimes? Or you were on your own pretty much?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody there can help you. But the-- the people's in the same situation.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were also poor.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the city helped you. In winter time, get you coal, because you warm up the house with the oven-- get you coal. And you know the blackness? It helped you a little, the welfare. Yeah. The [? joint ?] helped a little.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, did you go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me a little bit about what school was like for you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to school number 28, a public school. And this school is 21 [? Samonska ?] Street.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, my.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And I walk 3 kilometers on a one side to the school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you walk alone to school, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Alone.

speaker: interviewer
text: Alone. Now, once you got to school, were you mostly with Jewish children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only. Because before the war, is only boys-- no girls in this school, and Jewish boys. Girls at a different school. Before the war, boys and girls doesn't go in a one school-- separate.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, was the language that you were taught in Polish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Did you go to that school until the war? [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I go to the school to 14 years. Because seven grade-- only seven grade, the public school. I went [? there ?] a yeshiva.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this after school, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: After school. I have long [? payot. ?] I'm very orthodox.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the yeshiva like? Was it very strict?

speaker: interviewee
text: I learned the religion-- religion, you know, the Bible. No, like normal, like here-- the same thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: But it was pretty strict?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ah ha. You were taught--

speaker: interviewee
text: And there is the-- in Lublin, you have the biggest yeshiva in Europe. It's called Chachmei, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Yeshiva Chachmei Lublin. This mean in Hebrew, the Smart Yeshiva in Lublin-- in English. And this built in 1928, they built the yeshiva. [INAUDIBLE] and make like and built this in the same, like, in Israel, 2,000 years ago, in the [INAUDIBLE], by [INAUDIBLE]. I don't know in English how to--

speaker: interviewer
text: That's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. At the same day, for every student there are a room-- no luxury. And I remember in the house, my mother, my father, my brother talk, as one rich Polish man [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] get down for the pillows what he was [? doing. ?] [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And the rabbi said-- Rabbi Shapiro-- it was a--

speaker: interviewer
text: This was your teacher?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a da-- ah, biggest rabbi in Europe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh. What was his first name? Do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shapiro.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know his first name? Rabbi--

speaker: interviewee
text: I not remember that. I know in person about-- see, I walked in the street, into the synagogue and said, you not can use the down because the goose is not kosher. They take the down and sold this and bought kosher down. How little you know. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you go to the yeshiva most every day after school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everyday.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your sisters, did they study at all? Hebrew?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sis--

speaker: interviewer
text: Your sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody talked Hebrew.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father talked, you know? This was you learn there, in the yeshiva, or in the special schools-- a little school-- this was old Hebrew. It's called [NON-ENGLISH]. This was a little different like this Hebrew was in Israel. This is modern Hebrew in Israel. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: But your sisters generally did not attend any sort of Hebrew education?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, she in the house, a religion. And she-- she know-- the sister was living with my parents. And the sister was lived in Warsaw. It's not fanatic orthodox. She is modern orthodox-- modern. You know, religion. Don't wear the wigs like my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Your mother lived the life of a typical orthodox woman.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my father the same way.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, now as you were growing up in this household, obviously it was OK to be Jewish. There weren't any problems. But then you beg-- it began to change. Can we talk about that? Do you remember when things began to change, when being Jewish became less safe for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: What you mean?

speaker: interviewer
text: What are some of your earliest memories of trouble for Jews in your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: In my town, where I live, is no problems because we lived only Jew. Sleep Polish people too, only no problems. Lublin-- maybe other towns who sleep closer to [? Posnan-- ?] the Germany-- Germany people lived there, Polish Germany people. It's more trouble, a little, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, but then I guess some time in your childhood or your teens things began to change when the war began and the Nazis began to be felt. Oh, when the Nazis.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I guess what I'm trying to find out is, when and how did you begin to sense that there was trouble? Oh. In 1939, when the Germany come into Lublin-- this was the 17 days of September 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you remember that date?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: I remembered the town what I escape and everything. I could to tell you. Was it warm weather? Was it-- what was the weather like?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was September. September in Poland is a little cool. This is in nights, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: And were the high holidays already over? Do you remember that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it's not over, no, not over. The holidays is in October.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is September. And when they stopped [? here, ?] I remember where I live, everybody is not in the house. No, it's in the-- every houses have a gate you close. And everybody looked out. And at one-- my [? friend, ?] the name is Wasserman.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Somebody told that that the house where his sister live is afire. And this boy want to go to help there. And the Germany killed him in the street.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now--

speaker: interviewee
text: Because nobody can go out.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was all happened in one day?

speaker: interviewee
text: In one day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, at this point, what do you remember specifically? In your household, what were people saying? What were you told about this?

speaker: interviewee
text: When the German come to first day, when they come in the second day, is the people very, you know, afraid what could happen. Nobody-- no one knew what was going to happened. And every calling us from the streets. You have German with the special machine to kill. You can't run. You can walk slowly. And I--

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you at this time, just--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were 19, so--

speaker: interviewee
text: 18. And I remember one officer, a German officer, come to my father. He wants to fix the jacket, the [INAUDIBLE]. It's too big of him. And my father [INAUDIBLE] that's [? mended. ?] And the officer, the German officer, waiting in the house. And there is my sister's two girls-- and the boy with the girls, the twin. I take them. He kissed them and taked out from the pocket, his children from Germany, his children, and cried-- a German officer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this very early in the occupation when the Germans--

speaker: interviewee
text: This was-- this was the first week in the occupation.

speaker: interviewer
text: The first week?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I pay my father for the work. And I said, I don't like the work. I can do nothing. I have to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did that surprise you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Surprised me. Because before the war, I read the brown book what Hitler writes. It's called [POLISH] in Polish. And I find out there what going to happen-- what's happened in German with the Jew. And I remember somebody tell me that the Russian nine little tanks come to Germany to see him in the city hall, in Lublin. And I went to see the Russian tank for everybody. Then [INAUDIBLE] Russian is-- they're having there is everything good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Everything wonderful is Russian.

speaker: interviewee
text: Wonderful. And I will see the Russian. And when I work in center city, the Germany catched me-- a lot of young people and take them in a special place. This is Lipova 7.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, now just before we talk about that, I wonder if we could go back a little bit to that first two days when the Germans came. First of all, had you no idea that this was about to happen? Or did you have some idea?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody have idea of got killed to make the concentration camps. No, people read lot-- before I told you the brown book. Then the brown book Hitler write out everything what I'm going to do to the Jew. You understand what I mean?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a lot of people no-- a lot of people no believe this!

speaker: interviewer
text: How about your parents? Did they understand any of this? Did they anticipate any?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother-- my mother don't can go. Because she's sick. She asthma. My father don't-- father don't go because my mother no go. My father's, in this time, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] over the 50. My sister-- she had two little children. She not can go to Russia. A lot of people escaped. In the town, the people-- young people and older people, with children too. And my brother-in-law don't-- wanted to go, because my sister is with the children. When I'm-- I take me in the-- on the camp of Lipova 7, I'm there 13 month.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, again, I want to-- we want to talk about that. But I just wanted to go back one more time to the early days, when all of this was beginning. You mentioned the German officer coming to your house for your father to sew the coat. Were you, at that point, terrified of the Germans? Or did it feel-- I'm trying to get a sense of what that was like to have a German in your house. I like-- because on this time, I read a lot in my life. And I can-- my mind telling me this is a lot-- in every nations, you have good people and bad people. This man looked like very nice man, the officer. Like [? here, ?] you have a lot of officers like this, a lot of soldiers. Because in the Wehrmacht-- you know, the Wehrmacht? Mm hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Those are the regular army. There you're not seeing too-- you not can see too much anti-Semitism. But the SS, the Gestapo, you know-- and what they told the soldier, the soldier would do, you know? And other people, it's better [INAUDIBLE] they can get nothing-- kind of bread, and not milk, not-- it's the terrible. People died in the street.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, are you describing something that happened during the early time, when the Germans came to your city, when you couldn't get bread and you couldn't get food? That began right away.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right away.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. How did your family deal with that in the very beginning?

speaker: interviewee
text: For very hard. You see, you have a lot-- in this neighborhood, where I live, is a lot of Jewish stores-- little stores, with bread, with herring. I remember a special store, with the grocery. The people have a little and sold to you, you know, what I have to-- in the reserve. No-- very hard to get. A lot of people go to the farms. From Lublin to the farms are not so far.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: People walked to the farms-- 10 kilometer, 15 kilometer-- bring something for the family. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You also mentioned that some of the people in the town knew something was-- something bad was coming. And they managed to get to Russia to get away. Your family never talked about that, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: My family don't talk from this because she-- not-- not possible.

speaker: interviewer
text: It wasn't possible.

speaker: interviewee
text: Impossible. Impossible for him. My mother-- they can't go. She's a sick lady. She stay more on the bed like she walk.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have close relatives? Did your parents, for example, have a brother or a sister, or anybody?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the sister was living with my parents. My one sister with the-- the sons in Warsaw. And that's it. I have a lot of [INAUDIBLE] cousins. I have uncles. I have a-- aunts. No, you know, I'm [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they in Lublin with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lublin, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did many of them leave and go to Russia? Or did they all stay?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: You lost track of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Because when I take me in camp, and I escaped from there-- I escaped from there. It's impossible to escape. Two German officers-- it's get cold in winter time. This is in November, in beginning of November, in 1940.

speaker: interviewer
text: Comes in two officers, ask, who understand German? In school, I take German three years. And I speak German. Who know if you can to buy wool stocking? I know, I know. And take me out. And this camp is in the center of city from Lublin. And the two officers who walked-- and I am with him-- no cut the hand, nothing, you know? And there, in Lublin, no cars. In '39, you not have cars-- in '40, you didn't have cars in Poland. And-- I'm just going to stop you here. We're probably going to continue this on the next reel of tape, OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah ha.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're doing wonderful.

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe you want coffee?
